reasoning:
The question asks if there will be more than 10 times as many 'Violence against civilians' events in Zambia for the 30 days prior to three resolution dates in Nov 2025, Dec 2025, and Feb 2026, compared to the reference rate (1 + the 30-day average over the preceding 360 days). The current reference value as of late October 2025 is exactly 1.000, which mathematically implies that the 360-day average was precisely 0. This means there were 0 recorded events of 'Violence against civilians' in Zambia in the year spanning late October 2024 to October 2025. 

For the question to resolve YES, Zambia would need to experience 11 or more 'Violence against civilians' events in a single 30-day window. ACLED strictly defines these as violent events where an "organized armed group inflicts violence upon unarmed non-combatants." Zambia is a stable democracy with no active insurgencies, rebel groups, or ongoing civil conflicts. Violent incidences are generally sporadic street clashes, riots, or state repression, none of which strictly fit the criteria of "organized armed group inflicts violence upon unarmed non-combatants" unless party militias escalate significantly. 

Historically, such violence in Zambia has been extremely rare, and when it spikes, it is strongly tied to election cycles. The next general election is scheduled for August 13, 2026, which is more than six months after the final resolution date of February 7, 2026. Therefore, an unprecedented and sudden surge of an organized armed group resulting in 11+ attacks on civilians within 30 days is extremely improbable over this horizon. Predicting probabilities close to the floor (0.05 per rules, or down to 0.02 allowed limits) reflects the structural peace of the country and the lack of short-term catalysts. My submission is 3%, 4%, and 6%, scaling slightly up over time to account for growing but slight election-related uncertainty by early 2026.
